text: The surgeon uses an 18-inch Stratafix suture to penetrate the anterior abdominal wall just below the costal margin, aiming towards the right crus. The tip-up and arm four are positioned to elevate the liver without a formal liver retractor.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to pass through the right crus and returns to the anterior abdominal wall to retract the liver, achieving sufficient liver exposure for the operation.

A sponge is placed on the right side of the screen for easy access. The surgeon positions the tip-up instrument on the gastrocolic ligament, lifting the stomach to create tension. The distal dissection of the stomach begins, with plans to proceed upwards.

The surgeon uses a four D visage to retract the stomach, allowing for dissection along the gastro-splenic ligament. Arm four remains static to maintain tension, while the surgeon operates at a distance from the surgical field.
The surgeon drops a sponge into the surgical field, using it to retract and shift anatomical structures to the right side, creating a visual barrier that aids in organizing the operative area.

The surgeon exposes the left crus while the anesthesiologist retracts the Visi-G. Using arms three and four, the stomach is pulled to achieve the correct angle for the stapler. A bougie is passed down to ensure no obstruction, and the blue load is prepared for the first staple fire.

The surgeon positions the vessel sealer on arm three and uses arm four to lift the stomach, ensuring no posterior structures are caught. The tip-up instrument is positioned to continue stapling along the stomach.

The surgeon uses a stapler to create a staple line along the stomach, while employing a bipolar device to gently control mild bleeding at the distal end of the staple line. Arm four is positioned higher up on the stomach to assist in the procedure as the surgeon reassesses the stapler's progress.

The surgeon uses arm three to maneuver underneath the stomach, sliding the posterior fundus laterally while the bedside assistant prepares the stapler. Arm four is used to straighten the stomach, facilitating exposure for the next stapler bite.

The vessel sealer sweeps out, addressing minor bleeding on the specimen side. A white load stapler is introduced, and the surgeon carefully avoids stapling the fat pad while ensuring dissection reaches the left crus.
The surgeon removes a sponge from the surgical field to prevent it from being stapled or obstructing the view. A final staple is fired laterally to the fat pad.

As the stapler fires, the surgeon removes the vessel sealer and tip-up instrument. A SertaCell is placed on the staple line. The vessel sealer is reintroduced through arm four, while a leak test is conducted.
The surgeon places a SertaCell on the staple line. The vessel sealer is positioned in arm four to divide the stitch. The specimen and sponge are positioned for easy extraction.
